---
title: "weekly temperature tracker"
format: html
---
## Project Overview 
This project is a weekly temperature tracker that records daily high and low temperatures for a seven-day period in Oklahoma City. The program stores temperature values in lists, calculates summary statistics (minimum and maximum temperatures), and generates a line graph to visualize temperature trends across the week. The visualization also highlights the exact days on which the highest and lowest temperatures occur.

## Goals
The goal of this project is to collect daily high and low temperature data for one week in Oklahoma City, compute summary statistics (mean, minimum, and maximum), and visualize the data using a line graph. This allows us to observe patterns, compare temperature fluctuations, and identify trends in weather conditions throughout the week.

## Data Resources
Temperature data for Oklahoma City from 4/6/26 to 4/12/26 was collected from the National Weather Service website. No missing or estimated values were used. All data points were directly recorded from the selected date range. Daily high and low temperatures were manually recorded and stored in Python lists for analysis. 

## Final Code
```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

days = ["Mon","Tue","Wed","Thu","Fri","Sat","Sun"]

lows = [42, 42, 44, 51, 58, 59, 65]
highs = [73, 71, 76, 80, 83, 77, 82]

plt.figure()

# two lines only
plt.plot(days, highs, marker="o", label="Highs", color="red")
plt.plot(days, lows, marker="o", label="Lows", color="blue")

# combine for min/max
all_temps = highs + lows
all_days = days + days
all_values = highs + lows

min_temp = min(all_temps)
max_temp = max(all_temps)

min_index = all_values.index(min_temp)
max_index = all_values.index(max_temp)

# small highlights
plt.scatter(all_days[min_index], min_temp, s=200, color="black", label="Min Temp")
plt.scatter(all_days[max_index], max_temp, s=200, color="black", label="Max Temp")

plt.title("Weekly Temperatures for 4/6/26-4/12/26 in Oklahoma City")
plt.xlabel("Day")
plt.ylabel("Temperature (°F)")
plt.grid(True)
plt.legend()

plt.show()
```

## Code Explanation
The highs and lows lists store daily temperature values for one week. These lists are combined to compute the overall minimum and maximum temperatures using the min() and max() functions. These functions identify the lowest and highest values. 
The index() function identifies the position of these extreme values, allowing the corresponding day to be highlighted on the graph. The days list labels the x-axis so each temperature value corresponds to the correct day of the week. 
The plt.plot() function creates two lines representing high and low temperatures. The plt.scatter() function highlights the minimum and maximum temperatures using larger markers to make them distinct from the rest of the temperatures. 
Labels, a title, a grid, and a legend were added to improve readability and clarity of the graph.

## How to Run This Project
To run this project, the user needs Python installed along with the Matplotlib library. The code can be executed in Positron, Jupyter Notebook, or any Python-compatible environment. 
1. Enter or modify daily high and low temperatures in the highs and lows lists.
2. Run the code cells to calculate the minimum and maximum values 
4. View the generated graph showing temperature trends and extreme values for the week.

## Interpretation of Results:
The graph shows temperature variation over a one-week period in Oklahoma City. The graph from our final code shows a general upward trend in temperature over the course of the week. The highlighted points make it easy to identify the warmest and coolest days. The average from this week was observed at 64.5 degrees which can help us compare overall averages over weeks and months. This project demonstrates how basic data analysis and visualization techniques can be used to identify patterns and trends in real-world weather data. 

## Contributions
My contribution to this project included collecting and organizing the temperature data, writing and fixing the Python code, creating the data visualization using Matplotlib, and helping structure and write the notebook. 
My contribution to this project was helping collect the highs and the lows off of the National Weather Service website to create a graph and find the averages. I also helped interpret the results and how they apply in real world scientific situations.
